# Questions in Livestream 122

Live Stream #122: Myths, The Academic World, and Questions

Time links open the YouTube video at the relevant timestamp.

| Time | Question | Short answer / answer direction | Expanded answer |
|---:|---|---|---|
| <a href="https://youtu.be/chnlrXAizaM?t=535" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">8:55</a> | Is it true that Egyptians did not have a god of the sea, and if so why? | He says yes, broadly speaking, and ties that to Egypt's river-centered worldview rather than a maritime religious imagination like other Mediterranean cultures. | He explains that ordinary Egyptians had little sustained contact with the Mediterranean: the Delta coast was marshy, coastal settlements were limited, and most people lived along the Nile or in the southern Delta. Because the sea did not structure daily Egyptian life the way the Nile did, a sea god would not have had the same religious significance; instead, Egyptians had deities tied to the Nile and its animals, such as Hapi, Sobek, and Taweret. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/chnlrXAizaM?t=729" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">12:09</a> | What does he think of John Bergsma's *Pints with Aquinas* interview? | He says the New Testament and Gospel material is stronger than the Old Testament and archaeology claims, which he finds uneven. | He calls the interview a mixed bag: Bergsma was strongest when discussing his New Testament specialty, especially Gospel material, but Falk thought the Old Testament and archaeology portions lacked consistency. He agrees with some points, such as a late Exodus and a northwestern Sinai setting for the Reed Sea, but criticizes the use of chariot-at-the-bottom-of-the-sea claims and Steve Collins's Sodom material as too uncritical. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/chnlrXAizaM?t=1148" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">19:08</a> | Could the 480 years associated with Solomon's temple be symbolic, perhaps as 40 times 12? | He says it is possible to read the number typologically, though such a reading must stay controlled by context. | He says the suggestion is possible because 480 can signal a long period connected with the twelve tribes, but he cautions that in Judges the number 40 usually marks rest or peace rather than hardship. He notes that Kenneth Kitchen and others have tried to add up periods of rest to reach 480, and he thinks a symbolic connection to peace is more likely than a 480-year hardship scheme, since the recorded oppressions add up to far less. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/chnlrXAizaM?t=1327" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">22:07</a> | What can we really know about Pharaoh Narmer and even his odd catfish name? | He says we know very little securely and has to keep the answer cautious. | He explains that the catfish and chisel signs on Narmer's palette are not a descriptive name but a rebus, using images for their sounds to spell Narmer phonetically. Historically, he says the evidence is thin: the palette was found in a temple foundation deposit with early artifacts, Narmer's exact relationship to nearby kings is uncertain, and the palette mainly suggests warfare against Lower Egyptian groups and possible movement toward unification rather than a complete biography. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/chnlrXAizaM?t=1637" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">27:17</a> | Did Egyptians remember Narmer or Menes the way Britons remember King Arthur? | He says no; Menes was remembered as a founder, but not mythologized in the same way. | He says Narmer and the other Dynasty 0 or early Dynasty 1 kings did not live on in Egyptian popular legend the way Arthur did in Britain. Menes was remembered by later Egyptians as the founder of the country, but Falk frames that as a king-list or founding-memory tradition, not a rich heroic legend; the unresolved issue is whether Menes should be identified with Narmer or Aha. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/chnlrXAizaM?t=1884" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">31:24</a> | What percentage of Egyptian cities were named after kings? | He says it is hard to quantify without a toponym survey; cities could be named after kings, gods, or other things. | He treats the question as difficult because it would require a broad survey of Egyptian place names across periods. In the answer span he distinguishes royal naming from other naming patterns, noting that settlements and districts could be associated with kings, gods, geographic features, or administrative purposes, so he avoids giving a percentage without that kind of systematic evidence. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/chnlrXAizaM?t=1945" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">32:25</a> | Is it more common for Egyptian cities to be named after kings or gods? | He says both patterns occur, but frequency cannot be answered without a toponym survey. | He treats the follow-up as another survey question rather than giving a percentage. Egyptian cities could be named for kings, gods, or other features; he gives examples such as Baal-Zephon and Pithom for divine naming, Peramesses for royal naming, and Avaris as a name not based on either a god or a king. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/chnlrXAizaM?t=2103" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">35:03</a> | In Genesis 44:9-10, why do the brothers and steward negotiate punishment for Joseph's cup? | The brothers protest innocence with an oath, while Joseph has arranged the situation to keep Benjamin. | He says Joseph plants the divination cup as a pretext to detain the brothers because he wants the family brought safely to Egypt during the famine. The brothers are so certain none of them stole the cup that they offer death and slavery as an oath-like protest of innocence; Joseph then accepts the leverage but reduces the penalty to enslavement of the guilty party, knowing he has placed the cup with Benjamin and can use that crisis to draw Jacob to Egypt. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/chnlrXAizaM?t=2525" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">42:05</a> | Is there a connection between hardening the heart in Exodus and the Egyptian weighing of the heart? | He says only one of the main Hebrew terms lines up meaningfully with that imagery, so the connection should not be overstated. | He says the meaningful overlap is with *kavod*, the idea of being heavy or weighed down, not with all the Hebrew terms for hardening. In Egyptian judgment imagery, the heart is weighed against the feather of Ma'at, and a heart made heavy by wrongdoing is in danger; he allows that Israel may have picked up or shared this conceptual world in Egypt, but he limits the connection to the lexical and conceptual overlap around heaviness. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/chnlrXAizaM?t=2881" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">48:01</a> | Were Book of the Dead spells written by magicians who visited the underworld, like Orpheus? | He says no; they developed through layered mythological and ritual spell traditions. | He says there is no indication that the spells were authored by people who traveled through the underworld and returned. Instead, he describes a long development from Pyramid Texts, which reenact divine mythology for ritual benefit, into Coffin Texts and then the Book of the Dead, with later generations adding, expanding, and adapting mythological material into practical funerary spells. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/chnlrXAizaM?t=3113" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">51:53</a> | Why do dream interpretation and court dreams matter so much in the ancient Near East? | He says dreams were treated as politically and religiously significant, so interpreters like Joseph and Daniel could gain real influence. | He distinguishes Joseph and Daniel rather than treating them as the same case. For Joseph, Egyptian dream interpretation was codified in dream books, so Joseph's correct interpretation mattered because he contradicted the expected omen for the cupbearer and was later called when Pharaoh's dream was outside the standard books. Daniel's setting is different: Nebuchadnezzar tests his court experts by demanding both the dream and its interpretation, so Daniel's success exposes a unique divine ability rather than ordinary court technique. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/chnlrXAizaM?t=3742" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:02:22</a> | Is he going to play *Starfield* when it comes out? | He says he is strongly considering it, but may wait for reviews and bug fixes. | He says he has heard enough about *Starfield* to be interested and is considering playing it, though he has not made a firm decision. He adds that Bethesda had contacted him about a partner promotion, which he declined as outside the channel's focus, and that he prefers games unrelated to the ancient world for leisure. His main hesitation is practical: he wants to see reviews and may wait for patches because he expects a Bethesda release to have bugs. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/chnlrXAizaM?t=4061" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:07:41</a> | Did Potiphar believe his wife, and was Joseph imprisoned in Potiphar's house? | He says maybe, but Joseph would more likely be remanded to a Pharaoh-controlled prison rather than a palace prison in Potiphar's house. | He says the text does not let us know whether Potiphar believed his wife, and even belief would not necessarily mean immediate execution by Potiphar. In his reconstruction, a charge like this would be remanded upward for Pharaoh's judgment, while the prison would more likely be an Egyptian quarry or desert labor camp, not a barred palace prison in Potiphar's house; Potiphar's role as captain of the guard is tied to palace security, not owning a domestic prison. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/chnlrXAizaM?t=4608" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:16:48</a> | What should a first-year seminarian include when teaching Joshua? | He highlights Joshua's didactic movement from good tribes to bad tribes and its warning function. | He advises teaching Joshua as a didactic historical book rather than merely a chronological conquest account. The key pattern he points to is the movement from Judah, which mostly secures its inheritance, to Dan, which fails to secure its own land and steals someone else's; that order teaches how Israel's entrance into the land begins strongly and then moves from good to bad to worse, much like Judges later moves from better judges to worse ones. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/chnlrXAizaM?t=4972" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:22:52</a> | Could aliens learn lost-tech traditions from Egypt? | He jokes that they could only learn how to recreate the Bronze Age and rejects the ancient-alien premise as incoherent. | He answers the question by turning the lost-technology claim against itself: if aliens could cross many light-years, it would make little sense for them to come to Egypt either to learn or to teach how to make stone monuments with bronze chisels. He treats both directions of the claim as incoherent, then spends the rest of the segment riffing humorously on ancient-alien explanations rather than offering support for them. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/chnlrXAizaM?t=5375" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:29:35</a> | Can deontological ethics fit within the Bible? | He says yes; Scripture contains deontological commands along with virtue-ethics concerns. | He says the Bible contains both virtue ethics and deontological commands, so it should not be reduced to only one ethical model. Some passages extol virtues or ethical principles, while other commands function as direct divine imperatives; he uses food-law examples such as the prohibition on pork as a command with cultural background but not necessarily a simple virtue-ethics rationale. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/chnlrXAizaM?t=5530" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:32:10</a> | What is his view of Hebrews authorship? | He treats it as uncertain and speculative, with possibilities such as Apollos, Nicodemus, or Silas rather than Paul. | He stresses that Hebrews authorship is unknown and that any answer is speculation. He rejects Paul because Hebrews 2:3-4 seems to place the author among those who received the message from earlier witnesses, and he leans toward someone with deep Jewish learning and strong Greek ability, especially Apollos; Nicodemus or Silas remain possible, while he is less persuaded by Barnabas because the relevant Barnabas tradition is itself uncertain. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/chnlrXAizaM?t=6047" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:40:47</a> | Is there an Egyptian king or queen infamous like England's King John? | He says Khufu or Khafre are closest because of pyramid taxation traditions, while Menkaure was remembered more favorably. | After arguing that King John himself may be unfairly remembered, he says the closest Egyptian parallel would be a ruler infamous for heavy taxation and exhausting the country. Khufu is his main example because the Great Pyramid project was remembered as financially ruinous, with Khafre also having a tyrannical reputation, while Menkaure is contrasted as a more loved ruler who eased taxation and received popular offerings near his funerary complex. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/chnlrXAizaM?t=6780" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:53:00</a> | What does he think about the Nefertiti bust controversy? | He treats it as a politically charged issue involving both authenticity debates and Egyptian-German museum tensions. | He calls the bust beautiful and politically charged, then frames the ownership controversy around the old partage system at Amarna, where finds were divided between Egypt and the excavators. In his account, Egyptian officials had first choice and passed over the wrapped but available Nefertiti bust, likely undervaluing it compared with Akhenaten material; because he thinks they knew enough to inspect it, he views the later dispute more as regret over a bad selection than clear deception. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/chnlrXAizaM?t=7306" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:01:46</a> | What is his view of the Sothic cycle? | He accepts calendar drift but says the Sothic cycle is mostly useless for chronology. | He says Egypt's 365-day civil calendar really did drift against the solar year, with seasonal labels gradually becoming detached from weather until later intercalation. The Sothic cycle, however, is too uncertain for secure chronology in his view: observations could vary, the place and method of sighting are often unknown, and Egyptian uses of Sothic dates seem more tied to medicine and ritual timing than a chronological system. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/chnlrXAizaM?t=7685" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:08:05</a> | What is his favorite pre-modern Bible commentary? | He says the church fathers can help with historical theology, but old commentaries are limited for exegesis. | He names the early church fathers as the most valuable pre-modern commentary-like source, but mainly for historical theology and early reception rather than precise exegesis. He warns that older commentaries often lack the historical, linguistic, and archaeological knowledge gained in recent scholarship, and that older English or translated language can introduce false friends, so he uses them cautiously rather than as his main guide to what the biblical text means. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/chnlrXAizaM?t=8249" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:17:29</a> | How should modern readers think about Jewish concepts of time? | He says Israelite time is event-shaped, with linear recordkeeping present but repeated patterns and significant events mattering more than clock-like measurement. | He says Israelite time is event-shaped: linear time is present, but repeated covenant patterns and significant events matter more than clock-like measurement. Judges is his main example, where apostasy, oppression, deliverance, and peace recur in different regions as a kind of parallel cyclical pattern; he contrasts that with modern Western clock anxiety, where minutes and schedules dominate how people experience time. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/chnlrXAizaM?t=8770" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:26:10</a> | Were Sobek crocodile pools in palaces or only on temple grounds? | He says palace pools are not the normal context; temple or sacred-lake settings are more likely, especially at Sobek temples. | He says kings did not normally keep crocodiles in palace pools because crocodiles are dangerous, though palaces could have ornamental pools with fish and lotus plants. Sobek temples, especially in the Fayum, are the kind of special sacred-lake context where crocodiles might be intentionally kept, while crocodiles could also occasionally wander into ponds unintentionally, especially young ones moving inland. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/chnlrXAizaM?t=9176" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:32:56</a> | Did Egyptians get tattoos? | He says yes, especially religious motifs, and notes that imaging technology has helped identify tattoos on mummies. | He says evidence for tattoos has improved because photographic techniques can detect ink that normal mummy preservation and skin discoloration obscure. Tattoos appear especially among priestly figures and often use religious motifs such as ankhs, the Eye of Re, or Isis imagery; he also notes a tendency toward bilateral symmetry, with designs balanced on both sides of the body. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/chnlrXAizaM?t=9391" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:36:31</a> | How did Egyptians deal with skin cancer from the sun? | Tanning, ocher, and cosmetics helped somewhat, but he says skin cancer was still common. | He says many workers developed tans, and some people, especially in upper classes, used red ocher or cosmetics that could reduce sun exposure. Even so, he says ancient Egypt shows a high incidence of cancer, including cancers that metastasized to bone, and he does not think Egyptians necessarily understood the causal link between sun exposure and skin cancer. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/chnlrXAizaM?t=9595" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:39:55</a> | Is astrology witchcraft? | He says it is better categorized as divination when used to predict the future. | He says "witchcraft" is too vague for the category, and some astrology overlaps with observational astronomy. The condemned practice would be using the stars to predict the future, on the assumption that divine powers move or arrange them as signs of coming events; that makes it divination rather than the most precise example of witchcraft. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/chnlrXAizaM?t=9693" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:41:33</a> | What is a freewill offering in Exodus 35:29? | He describes it as a voluntary extra contribution for the tabernacle, beyond required taxation. | He explains Exodus 35:29 as a voluntary contribution for the tabernacle from those whose hearts moved them to bring materials. Unlike required taxation or tithes, a freewill offering is not compelled, has no specified amount or item, and is given out of gratitude, thankfulness, or desire to advance the work. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/chnlrXAizaM?t=10566" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:56:06</a> | Do Calvinists generally hold deterministic or fatalistic views? | He says classical Calvinism does not, and distinguishes it from hyper-Calvinist or neo-Calvinist distortions. | He says classical Calvinism is not fatalism or determinism, though he criticizes some modern or hyper-Calvinist presentations for drifting that way. In his account, classical Calvinism still includes a kind of human freedom, including the freedom to rebel, and perseverance is not God forcing someone along a path regardless of will; he places Calvin closer to Augustine and Luther than to some modern Calvinist rhetoric. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/chnlrXAizaM?t=11451" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:10:51</a> | Could some Scripture preserve temple inscriptions like Egyptian temple inscriptions? | He says yes, especially 1 Kings 6:1 as a likely temple dedication inscription. | He says 1 Kings 6:1 almost certainly preserves the wording of a temple dedication inscription because it has the formulaic date-and-building structure known from ancient Near Eastern dedications. He compares the practice with Egyptian dedication stelae and adds that the Hezekiah tunnel inscription provides another example of an Israelite monumental inscription paralleling biblical material, though he is more tentative about other possible temple texts such as Psalms. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/chnlrXAizaM?t=12129" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:22:09</a> | Is there a focused effort in Egyptology or biblical studies to refute the Bible? | He says Egyptology mostly does not care, while some biblical-studies advocacy is skeptical or anti-biblical. | He says Egyptology as a field is mostly indifferent to the Bible, often seeing Egyptian texts and culture as the main object of interest rather than trying to refute Israelite history. Biblical studies is different in his view: some scholars and programs develop a skeptical or anti-biblical advocacy posture, though he also notes a broad middle with varying degrees of skepticism and acceptance. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/chnlrXAizaM?t=12545" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:29:05</a> | What is the best translation for Hebrew *ra'ah* in Yahweh-yireh? | Context determines the translation; "see" is basic, but the semantic range includes provide, show, or reveal. | He explains that the root has a base sense of seeing, but Hebrew words often have broad lexical ranges, and this one can extend to understand, reveal, inspect, look after, choose, present oneself, and by extension provide. For Yahweh-yireh, he argues that no single gloss should be imposed mechanically; the context determines whether "see," "provide," "show," or another range meaning best communicates the Hebrew. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/chnlrXAizaM?t=13873" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:51:13</a> | What language did Israelites speak in Egypt and after the Exodus? | He suggests an Amorite or Amoritic language in Egypt, with a shift toward Hebrew during or after the wilderness period. | He says the Israelites likely descended from Amorite-speaking groups and probably spoke an Amorite or Amoritic dialect while in Egypt, pointing to Amoritic names at Avaris as supporting evidence. During the Sinai period, he suggests that this speech gradually developed toward classical Hebrew, with later biblical texts also undergoing spelling and grammar updating as the language changed. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/chnlrXAizaM?t=14064" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:54:24</a> | Can the Ten Commandments change meaning in other languages or translations? | Not legitimately, though translations can ideologically distort meaning. | He says the commandments should not legitimately change meaning in translation, but translations can distort meaning through ideology, sectarian preference, or misuse of Hebrew grammar. His examples include turning declarative statements into questions where context does not require it, or selecting a least-likely meaning from a word's semantic range to fit a theological or cultural agenda. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/chnlrXAizaM?t=14566" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:02:46</a> | How do we know how to pronounce the divine name, and is "Yahuwah" correct? | He says comparative Semitic evidence favors Yahweh rather than Yahuwah. | He says the consonants are not the main uncertainty: yod, he, waw, and final he are identifiable, and the waw is probably a consonant rather than a vowel in this case. The vowel pattern is reconstructed through comparative Semitics, especially related Semitic languages with clearer vowel patterns, leading him to favor a two-syllable pronunciation like Yahweh and to reject Yahuwah as unlikely. |
